channel.title: DevOps on Larry James Henry
channel.description: Recent content in DevOps on Larry James Henry

item.title: Azure Platform Engineering: The Complete Guide to Building an Enterprise Landing Zone
item.description:
Cloud adoption at enterprise scale fails when it lacks a foundation. Without a structured landing zone, subscriptions proliferate as unmanaged silos, security policies are applied inconsistently, and networking becomes a chaotic web of peerings and overlapping IP ranges. “Just create a subscription and start building” is a strategy that leads to a $50,000 month-end surprise and a security audit you won’t pass.

An Azure Landing Zone (ALZ) is the architectural anchor for your cloud estate. It provides the shared services — networking, identity, governance, and logging — that every application needs but no single team should manage alone. In 2026, the standard for building these foundations has shifted from monolithic modules to the Azure Verified Modules (AVM) initiative, emphasizing “Metadata-First” architectures and secret-less CI/CD.

item.title: Subscription Vending: Automating New Workload Onboarding with IaC
item.description: Cloud adoption at scale fails when the platform team is a manual bottleneck. If onboarding a new application team requires a Jira ticket, three emails, and 10 business days of manual configuration, you haven’t built a platform—you’ve built a bureaucracy. In an enterprise landing zone, we treat the Subscription as a product. We use a Subscription Vending pattern to automate the creation and configuration of every new environment through a pull request.

item.title: CI/CD Pipeline for Your Landing Zone: Deploying Azure Verified Modules with GitHub Actions
item.description:
Deploying an enterprise landing zone from a local terminal is a single point of failure. It lacks an audit trail, bypasses peer review, and relies on long-lived administrative secrets stored on personal machines. A production-grade foundation requires a GitOps approach, where every change to the infrastructure is managed as code, tested in a pipeline, and deployed via a secure, secret-less automation identity.

The Landing Zone CI/CD Pipeline transforms your foundation from a collection of scripts into a reliable, repeatable platform service. In 2026, the standard for these pipelines is Workload Identity Federation (OIDC), which eliminates static API keys, and Shift-Left Testing using tools like PSRule. This guide teaches you how to build a production pipeline using GitHub Actions to deploy Azure Verified Modules (AVM) and establish a secure deployment perimeter for your landing zone.
